findings.title: Unchecked null return from store.get(token) in requireAuth
findings.description: store.get(token) on line 24 can return null or undefined when the token is invalid, expired, or not found in the session store. Line 26 immediately accesses session.userId and session.permissions without any null guard. This causes a TypeError crash, and because the error is unhandled, next() is never called with an error -- the request hangs or crashes the process depending on the Express error handling setup.
findings.triggerScenario: Any request with an invalid or expired Bearer token -> store.get() returns null -> line 26 throws TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'userId') -> 500 error instead of 401
findings.suggestedFix: Add a null check after store.get(): if (!session) { return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Invalid or expired token' }); }

findings.title: Undefined token passed to store.get() in refreshSession
findings.description: On line 44, token is extracted using optional chaining (req.headers.authorization?.replace('Bearer ', '')), which yields undefined if the authorization header is missing. On line 48, this potentially undefined value is passed directly to store.get(token). Depending on the store implementation, this may throw, return unexpected data, or cause a key collision (e.g., storing/fetching under the key 'undefined').
findings.triggerScenario: Request to refresh endpoint with no Authorization header -> token is undefined -> store.get(undefined) -> unpredictable behavior (crash, wrong session lookup, or silent failure caught by the overly broad catch block)
findings.suggestedFix: Add an early return if token is falsy: if (!token) { return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Missing authorization header' }); }

findings.title: Inverted expiration check extends already-expired sessions instead of valid ones
findings.description: Line 50 checks session.expiresAt < Date.now(), which is true when the session has ALREADY expired. The code then extends these expired sessions (line 51). Sessions that are still valid (expiresAt > Date.now()) are never extended. This is inverted logic: expired sessions get a new lease on life, while valid sessions approaching expiration are ignored. From a security perspective, this means an attacker with an expired session token can call the refresh endpoint and get the session extended, effectively bypassing session expiration.
findings.triggerScenario: User's session expires -> user calls /refresh -> condition session.expiresAt < Date.now() is true -> expired session gets extended by 1 hour -> user regains access with an expired token. Meanwhile, active users whose sessions are about to expire cannot refresh them.
findings.suggestedFix: Invert the condition to extend sessions that are still valid but approaching expiration: if (session && session.expiresAt > Date.now()) { ... } -- or if the intent is to renew expired sessions within a grace period, add a grace window check.

findings.title: TOCTOU race condition in deductCredits allows double-spending
findings.description: deductCredits performs a read (getBalance on line 34), a check (line 36), and a write (updateBalance on line 39) as three separate async operations with no atomicity guarantee. If two concurrent requests call deductCredits for the same user, both can read the same balance, both pass the sufficiency check, and both deduct -- resulting in a balance that went negative, effectively allowing double-spend of credits.
findings.triggerScenario: User has 100 credits. Two concurrent requests each try to deduct 80 credits. Both read balance=100, both pass the check (100 >= 80), both write balance=20. User spent 160 credits but only had 100. Final balance is 20 instead of the expected -60 (or rejection of the second request).
findings.suggestedFix: Use an atomic compare-and-swap operation, a database transaction with row-level locking (SELECT FOR UPDATE), or an optimistic concurrency control pattern (version field). E.g.: await store.deductBalanceAtomic(userId, amount) that does the check-and-deduct in a single atomic operation.

findings.title: refreshSession catch block swallows errors and lies about success
findings.description: The catch block on line 53 logs only 'refresh failed' without including the error object (e is captured but never used). After the catch, execution falls through to line 57 which returns { status: 'ok' } regardless of whether the refresh actually succeeded. This means: (1) the caller believes the refresh worked when it did not, (2) debugging is impossible because the actual error is discarded, and (3) any store failure (connection down, corruption) is silently hidden.
findings.triggerScenario: Session store becomes unavailable (e.g., Redis connection drops) -> store.get() throws -> catch logs 'refresh failed' with no details -> response is { status: 'ok' } -> client believes session was refreshed -> session actually expires -> user gets kicked out unexpectedly on next request.
findings.suggestedFix: Log the actual error: console.error('refresh failed', e). Return an error response in the catch block: return res.status(500).json({ error: 'Session refresh failed' }). Or at minimum, set a different status code so the caller knows the operation failed.

findings.title: hasPermission ignores the permission parameter -- non-admin users always denied
findings.description: The hasPermission function accepts a permission string parameter but never evaluates it. For admin users, it returns true (line 66). For ALL other roles (user, viewer), it unconditionally returns false (line 68). The permission parameter is entirely unused. This means no granular permission checking exists -- the function is equivalent to isAdmin(). Any code relying on hasPermission('read') or hasPermission('write') to grant access to non-admin users will always deny them, regardless of their actual permissions.
findings.triggerScenario: A user with role 'user' has permission 'read-reports' in their session.permissions array. Code calls hasPermission(user, 'read-reports') expecting true. Function returns false because user.role is not 'admin'. User is denied access to reports they should be able to see.
findings.suggestedFix: Check the user's permissions or session data for the requested permission. For example: if (user.role === 'admin') return true; return user.permissions?.includes(permission) ?? false; -- noting that the User interface may need a permissions field, or the check should be done against the session store.

discarded_findings.title: Loose equality (==) instead of strict equality (===) in hasPermission
discarded_findings.reason_discarded: Below vote threshold (1 < 2). Only flagged by Pass 1. While technically a code smell, both operands are strings in practice so the behavior is identical to ===.

discarded_findings.title: Token extraction does not validate Bearer scheme prefix
discarded_findings.reason_discarded: Below vote threshold (1 < 2). Only flagged by Pass 2. The replace('Bearer ', '') approach is common and functional, even if not robust against non-Bearer schemes.
